Empires & Puzzles Hohenwerf Review: The Ferocious Gargoyle of Mindless Misery
Hohenwerf brings fast mana control and annoying fiends to the table, but can his stony skin protect him from modern stat-monsters? We break down the Ferocious Gargoyle's kit and viability.
Hohenwerf Review: Is This Mind-Controlling Statue Still Rock Solid?
Hohenwerf: The Fiendish Gargoyle of Mindless Misery

challenge event. Released back in September 2022, he entered the game as a premier fast-speed sniper with a nasty trick up his stony sleeve: the ability to force enemies into a "Mindless Attack" loop via a unique Fiend. While he lacks a costume to update his wardrobe (and stats), his base kit remains mechanically distinct. Think of him as a statue that comes to life not to protect the garden, but to relentlessly annoy your neighbors until they punch their own friends.

Hohenwerf looks like he woke up on the wrong side of the cathedral. He is a hulking, winged beast with a lion-esque face and massive horns, rendered in stone-grey tones with ominous red accents on his armor. It’s a classic "Gargoyle" aesthetic—imposing, ancient, and very much ready to drop a rock on someone's head. Unlike the more elegant heroes of the opera or the beach party, Hohenwerf is here to remind us that sometimes, you just need a big, angry monster.
Let's not sugarcoat it: by 2026 standards, Hohenwerf's stats are relics of a bygone era.
In a world where new attackers push 1300+ base attack, 805 is considered low. Even with Limit Breaks, he struggles to compete with the raw damage of modern snipers.
These defensive stats are now "paper-thin." A modern sniper breath could accidentally shatter him.
passive (detailed below) is the only thing keeping him relevant. Without it, he would be unplayable in top-tier content.

It absorbs 30% of the target's max HP in healing. This makes removing the fiend (and thus the Mindless Attack status) difficult without a dedicated overhealer or minion destroyer.
After 4 turns, the fiend pops for 150 damage. This is negligible damage, but it means the control is not infinite. However, 4 turns of potential lockdown at Fast speed is excellent.

Upon release, Hohenwerf was a tactical monster. The ability to lock down "Overpowered" slow heroes (like Alfrike or Khufu) at Fast speed made him a top-tier attacker.
The stat gap is undeniable. Modern heroes have nearly double his effective bulk and damage. His 450% hit is no longer a threat; it's the Fiend that matters.
He is no longer viable on
; he's too easy to kill with tile damage before he fires, and minion counters are everywhere.
, he remains a potent tool for
where you need to shut down a specific, dangerous target (like a reviver) quickly. He is a surgeon's scalpel, not a sledgehammer.
As of this review, Hohenwerf does not have a costume.
This is his biggest drawback. Without the stat boost from a costume bonus, he falls further behind with every new hero release.
Because Hohenwerf relies entirely on his Special Skill to survive (via Stoneskin) and be useful (via Fiend), staying alive is priority number one.
Maximizes his chances of surviving the initial board movement to fire his skill once. Once he fires, Stoneskin takes over.
His snipe damage becomes negligible, serving only as a delivery system for the Fiend.

Extremely risky. With ~1500 base HP, a modern hero breathing on him might kill him before he gets 7 tiles.

drain their mana (via Mindless Attack) at Fast speed is elite utility.
Once active, he becomes temporarily invincible to direct damage, allowing him to tank hits that would kill modern heroes.
The fiend absorbs healing, making it doubly effective against healers (who can't heal it off easily) and preventing them from cleansing the effect.

Monks, Clerics, and heroes with "Resist Ailments" can resist the Mindless Attack effect or the Fiend itself.
Modern heroes who destroy minions/fiends or prevent them (like Lunar New Year heroes) completely neuter his kit.
Unlike the original Mindless Attack (which lasts until mana is full), the Fiend expires after 4 turns, meaning the control isn't truly "infinite."
Excellent for neutralizing a specific threat (e.g., a Mother North wing or a Khufu flank).

If you pulled him early, rejoice! He is a game-changer for map stages and early raiding. His stoneskin will let him cheese bosses that should surely kill him. Level him immediately; he will teach you the value of mana control.

Hohenwerf is a luxury tool. Do not Limit Break him (especially LB2) unless you lack modern red options. His utility works at 4/80 for the most part. If you face a team with 6000+ Team Power, leave him on the bench unless you can protect him or guarantee 7 red tiles instantly.
When first previewed, players feared an "infinite mindless attack loop." The 4-turn explosion limit was added to prevent him from permanently locking down a hero with zero counterplay.

(Green). The consensus was Hohenwerf is better because "he hits harder and doesn't give mana to the enemy," though Zocc's control is technically separate from a fiend (so harder to block with anti-minion tech).
"I hate gargoyles... mindless attack... alfrike is super light."
- A frustrated summoner realizing the annoyance potential.

Hohenwerf is the definition of "Old but Gold" mechanics trapped in a "Bronze Age" body. His ability to turn an enemy's mana bar against them is timeless, but his stats are undeniably dusty. He is a fantastic hero to have in your depth chart for War, ready to tag in when you see a reviver that needs to be silenced
. Just don't ask him to arm-wrestle the new dragons or goblins; he'll crumble.
A tactical gem for offense, but keep him away from the defense team.
A detailed review of Hohenwerf, the 5-star Fire Gargoyle from Empires & Puzzles. Analyze his fast mana control, stoneskin passive, and modern viability in a power-crept meta.
